hook: In 12 months, no US state will regulate AI developers independently
headline: White House AI Framework Preempts State Laws
body: On March 20, the White House released a four-page document that will reshape AI governance in America for the next decade. The National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence is not just guidance. It is a legislative blueprint asking Congress to strip states of their power to regulate AI development.

The framework's most consequential provision: federal preemption. States would be barred from imposing liability on AI developers for how third parties use their models. If someone deploys an AI system to commit fraud, the company that built the model walks free under state law. California's SB 1047, Colorado's AI Act, and dozens of pending state bills would be effectively nullified overnight.

The seven-pillar framework covers child safety, intellectual property, free speech, innovation, workforce development, and community protection. But the through-line is unmistakable: existing sector-specific regulators and industry-led standards, not a new federal AI authority, will set the rules. The document explicitly opposes 'open-ended liability' that could create 'excessive litigation' for AI companies.

Critics argue this creates an accountability vacuum. If states cannot hold developers liable and no new federal body exists to do so, who polices AI harms? Proponents counter that regulatory fragmentation across 50 states is strangling innovation. The reality is more nuanced: the framework preserves state authority over child safety, fraud, consumer protection, and government procurement of AI.

This is the clearest signal yet that Washington views AI companies as strategic national assets, not entities to be constrained. Whether that trade-off serves the public interest is the defining policy debate of 2026.
insight: The framework's real power is in what it prevents, not what it creates. By preempting state AI laws while declining to build a federal enforcement body, Washington is constructing a regulatory vacuum that benefits incumbents with the most resources to self-govern.
caption:
The White House just told 50 states to stop regulating AI developers.

Its new National Policy Framework asks Congress to preempt state AI laws, shield developers from third-party misuse liability, and rely on industry self-governance instead of a new federal authority.

Child safety gets a carve-out. Innovation gets a blank check. Accountability gets... a question mark.

This four-page document will shape AI law for the next decade. The question is: who does the accountability vacuum actually serve?

What's your read on federal preemption of state AI laws?
